lesson_title: Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.front: When a member function is called on a derived class object, where does the compiler look first?
cards.back: The compiler first looks in the derived class. If not found there, it walks up the inheritance chain checking each parent class.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.text: To modify the way a function defined in a base class works in the derived class, simply {{c1::redefine the function in the derived class}}.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: The derived class has its own identify() function, so that one is called instead of the base class version.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.text: To call a base class function from a derived class function with the same name, use: {{c1::Base::functionName()}}
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.explanation: Calling identify() without scope resolution defaults to the current class (Derived::identify()), which calls itself infinitely. Should use Base::identify() instead.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.front: When you redefine a function in a derived class, does it inherit the access specifier from the base class?
cards.back: No, it uses whatever access specifier it is defined under in the derived class. A private function in base can be redefined as public in derived, or vice versa.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.text: The compiler will select the best matching function from the {{c1::most-derived}} class with at least one function with that name.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.question: Which print() function is called?
cards.explanation: Because there's at least one print() in Derived, the compiler only considers Derived's functions. Base::print(int) is not even considered, so 5 is implicitly converted to double.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.text: To make all base class functions with a given name visible in the derived class for overload resolution, use: {{c1::using Base::functionName;}}
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.concept: Calling base class friend functions from derived class
cards.explanation: Friend functions aren't part of the class, so scope resolution (Base::) won't work. Instead, use static_cast to temporarily cast the Derived object to a Base reference.
examples: This calls the Base version of operator<<
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.front: What happens when a derived class redefines a base class function?
cards.back: The derived function hides the base class function. To call the base version, you must use scope resolution (Base::functionName()) or cast to Base.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.question: How does 'using Base::print;' affect overload resolution?
cards.explanation: The using declaration makes all Base::print functions visible in Derived for overload resolution, so now Base::print(int) is considered and selected as the best match for integer 5.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior

cards.front: If a derived class has no function with a given name, where does the compiler look next?
cards.back: It walks up the inheritance chain, checking each parent class in turn, until it finds a matching function or reaches the top of the hierarchy.
cards.source: 24.7 - Calling inherited functions and overriding behavior
